parsed.message:
Your geyser appears to have a corroded tank. This is indicated by the rusty brown water visible in the drip tray and your report of increased electricity bills and hot water running out faster than before.

This issue typically develops when the internal sacrificial anode, designed to protect the geyser tank from rust, has been fully consumed over time. Once the anode is gone, the tank's steel walls begin to corrode, leading to rust in the water and eventual leaks.

If left unaddressed, the corrosion will worsen, increasing the risk of a catastrophic tank failure and a significant flood. This can cause extensive water damage to your property and potentially lead to electrical hazards if water comes into contact with wiring.

parsed.message:
Your garage door appears to have a missing or broken counterbalance spring on one side. This spring is crucial for balancing the weight of the door, allowing the motor to open and close it smoothly with minimal effort. Without this spring, the full weight of the door is unevenly distributed, causing it to sag on one side and placing excessive strain on the opener motor.

Counterbalance springs, whether torsion or extension types, are designed to last for a certain number of cycles. Over time, the metal fatigues and can snap, often with a loud noise. Once one spring fails, the door's balance is severely compromised, leading to the symptoms you described where the door opens partially, the motor beeps, and then it closes again.

If left unaddressed, the continuous strain on the garage door opener motor will likely cause it to burn out prematurely. The uneven load can also bend or damage other components, such as the lifting cables, rollers, tracks, or the door panels themselves, leading to more extensive and costly repairs.

Operating a garage door with a missing or broken spring can be dangerous. The door is extremely heavy and can fall unexpectedly, causing serious injury or damage to property. It is best to avoid attempting to operate the door manually or via the motor until it has been professionally repaired.
image_descriptions: This image shows the left side of the garage door, which is visibly dropped and angled downwards. The lifting arm and track are visible, but there is no counterbalance spring present on this side, indicating its absence.

The full view of the garage door reveals a significant tilt, with the left side much lower than the right. The ceiling-mounted opener motor and its rail are visible, with the door's uneven position clearly evident.

A close-up of the central attachment point between the garage door and the opener motor's rail is shown. The connecting rod appears intact, but the uneven pull on the door is apparent due to the overall misalignment.

This image provides a detailed view of the connecting bracket on the top edge of the garage door, where it attaches to the opener's drive arm. The bracket itself appears undamaged, but the door's overall sag is still noticeable from this angle.

contractor_checklist: Inspect the garage door system to identify the exact type of counterbalance spring (torsion or extension) and confirm its failure.
Replace the missing or broken spring with a new one appropriate for the door's weight and dimensions.
Rebalance the garage door to ensure it operates smoothly and evenly.
Check all associated hardware, including lifting cables, rollers, and tracks, for any secondary damage caused by the imbalance.
parsed.homeowner_prep: Keep the garage door disengaged from the opener motor and avoid attempting to open or close it until a technician arrives.
parsed.diy_verification: With the motor disengaged using the emergency release cord, attempt to lift the door manually; if it feels extremely heavy on one side and does not stay balanced, the spring system is faulty.

Visible severe tilt of the garage door in Image 2

Symptoms described by user (motor beeps, partial opening) are consistent with spring failure

Droplets are visible on the brass T-piece fitting, indicating an active leak. The second image shows water staining on the wooden support structure below the pipe penetrations. parsed.message:
The standing water in your drip tray has a distinct rusty brown colour, which indicates that the internal steel cylinder of your geyser has corroded and begun to leak. When the inner tank rusts through, water escapes into the outer casing and pools in the safety tray below.

This internal corrosion typically develops over several years as the sacrificial anode rod inside the tank degrades. Once the anode is completely spent, the water begins to attack the steel tank itself, leading to structural failure of the cylinder and heavy mineral scaling on the heating element, which explains your rising electricity bills and lack of hot water.

If left unaddressed, a corroded cylinder can fail catastrophically, leading to significant water damage to your ceilings and floors if the drip tray overflows or the leak worsens suddenly.
parsed.action_required:

Once the anode is completely spent, the water begins to attack the steel tank itself, leading to structural failure of the cylinder and heavy mineral scaling on the heating element, which explains your rising electricity bills and lack of hot water.

If left unaddressed, a corroded cylinder can fail catastrophically, leading to significant water damage to your ceilings and floors if the drip tray overflows or the leak worsens suddenly.

parsed.message:
The garage door is missing its left-hand tension spring, which is a critical part of the counterbalance system. Without this spring, the door is far too heavy for the motor to lift on its own. When you attempt to open it, the motor detects the excessive weight as an obstruction, triggers its safety overload protection, beeps, and reverses to prevent damage.

These heavy-duty tension springs are under immense force and are designed to balance the weight of the heavy timber door. When one spring is missing or snaps, the entire load shifts to the remaining spring and the motor arm, which can quickly cause secondary damage to the tracks or the motor itself.
parsed.action_required:

These heavy-duty tension springs are under immense force and are designed to balance the weight of the heavy timber door. When one spring is missing or snaps, the entire load shifts to the remaining spring and the motor arm, which can quickly cause secondary damage to the tracks or the motor itself.
